lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market slaughter and meat-processing operators that repeatedly provide custom, toll, or inspected processing services to livestock owners, farms, brands, and other external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Large integrated packers, firms principally buying livestock and selling meat on their own account, captive plants, poultry slaughter, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring or repeat business-to-business and owner-paid processing, commonly billed as a slaughter fee plus a per-pound processing charge, with optional packaging and value-added service fees.
lens.deviation_from_default: The lens is narrowed to custom and toll slaughter/processing because NAICS 311611 combines fee-for-service processors with integrated commodity packers whose principal revenue is product sales; mixing the two would make retention and eligibility incoherent.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader 311600 occupation mix rather than wage-weighted AI task exposure for 311611. USDA project descriptions identify automatable cutting, monitoring, logging, and compliance workflows; judgment maps those workflows onto the broader-industry employment mix. This likely overstates exposure for small custom plants with variable carcasses and understates exposure in scheduling, documentation, and inspection support.
a.rationale: The occupation mix is dominated by physical production work: cutters/trimmers and slaughterers/meat packers alone are 30.84% of broader-industry employment, while food-processing workers are 39.85%. AI-enabled vision, sensing, documentation, scheduling, and selective robotics can address part of this work, but dexterous variable-product handling and direct animal work constrain the exposed share.
a.change_evidence: A 311611-specific payroll-by-occupation extract joined to task-level time studies at small custom plants, plus vendor-verified deployment data for vision, robotic cutting, scheduling, and HACCP documentation.
caveats: OEWS is NAICS 311600, includes poultry and processing, and is employment-weighted rather than wage-weighted.
USDA automation projects demonstrate technical targets, not commercial adoption or realized labor removal.

rho.bridge: USDA research descriptions report limited robotic uptake and manual data collection among U.S. meat processors, but do not measure five-year implementation of the defined exposed opportunity. The range translates stated cost, infrastructure, usability, safety, and precision barriers into an implementation share; it may understate adoption if low-cost vision and workflow software diffuse faster than full robotics.
rho.rationale: Monitoring, logging, alerts, scheduling, and administrative workflows can be implemented faster than carcass manipulation. Limited historic robotics uptake, high infrastructure needs, strict safety and quality tolerances, and scarce small-plant IT capacity keep the five-year implemented fraction well below the technical exposure.
rho.change_evidence: A cohort study of small and midsize slaughter plants tracking automation projects from quote through production, including capex, installation time, downtime, rejection rates, labor hours saved, and 24-month persistence.
caveats: Grant project descriptions may emphasize unmet needs and anticipated benefits.
Implementation varies sharply between digital compliance tools and physical robotics.

e.bridge: North Carolina's government guide documents that custom facilities process owner-supplied animals and that processors serve farms for fees, but it does not measure the national share of LMM 311611 firms using that model. The estimate applies this observed model to the LMM population; it may be biased upward if many small firms primarily sell meat on their own account or downward where toll processing is not separately disclosed.
e.rationale: Custom and inspected fee-for-service slaughter clearly fits the external recurring-service lens, while integrated packers and principal-account product sellers do not. Small processors commonly serve multiple farms, but no national revenue-model split was located, so eligibility is bounded broadly.
e.change_evidence: FSIS establishment-directory records matched to company websites, customer contracts, and revenue segmentation for a stratified sample of the 177 estimated LMM firms, coding toll/custom revenue share and customer concentration.
caveats: The source is a North Carolina guide rather than a national census.
Custom-exempt and federally inspected fee-for-service operations have different addressable customers and economics.

s5.rationale: A five-year control-transfer probability near one-fifth is a bounded judgment for small, asset-heavy, often owner-operated processors. Succession pressure and strategic interest support transfers, while environmental, food-safety, labor, livestock-supply, and facility-capex liabilities can instead produce closure or nonqualifying asset sales.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction and closure census built from PitchBook/Capital IQ, state inspection-license changes, FSIS grant-of-inspection changes, UCC filings, and interviews with specialist food-plant brokers, explicitly distinguishing control sales from closures and internal reorganizations.
caveats: No industry-specific denominator-based transfer study was found.
A license or establishment-name change does not necessarily represent a qualifying control transfer.

q.bridge: A state agriculture guide observes fee architecture, and USDA observes concentration, capacity economics, entry, and price-spread behavior in broader meatpacking. Neither directly measures five-year retention of AI benefits in LMM custom processors. The range infers pass-through from fixed/per-pound menus, local capacity, and competitive repricing; broader packer concentration may overstate small-plant pricing power.
q.rationale: A flat slaughter fee plus per-pound processing charges do not automatically pass labor savings through, allowing initial retention. Over renewals, customers can demand better pricing and competitors can copy workflow tools, but constrained local capacity, specialized inspection status, transport radius, and value-added services should preserve a meaningful share.
q.change_evidence: Customer-level invoice panels and contracts for custom processors before and after automation, paired with local competitor price lists, capacity utilization, renewal timing, and contribution-margin bridges.
caveats: Published fee structures do not reveal discounts, utilization, or contract duration.
Large-packer market concentration is an imperfect proxy for local custom-processing competition.

d5.bridge: USDA projects national meat consumption and exports, not constant-price demand for outsourced non-poultry slaughter services over exactly 2026-2031. The range translates modest projected beef and pork consumption growth plus pork export growth into service throughput; it may overstate custom-processor demand if growth concentrates in integrated plants and understate it if regional processing capacity remains scarce.
d5.rationale: USDA's baseline expects 2033 domestic per-capita beef and pork consumption above 2024 and substantial pork export growth. Population growth supports total throughput, offset by cattle-cycle volatility, substitution toward poultry, plant closures, and the possibility that volume accrues to integrated packers rather than eligible service operators.
d5.change_evidence: USDA baseline tables for 2026-2031 beef, pork, veal, lamb, and goat commercial slaughter volumes combined with Census population, FSIS establishment-level slaughter data, and custom-processor booking/utilization histories.
caveats: The projection includes poultry in some comparisons, while this code excludes poultry.
Commodity production and exports are only proxies for custom/toll service demand.

o.bridge: FSIS and state guidance establish physical inspection, handling, processing, packaging, and labeling obligations, while USDA robotics research describes human-in-the-loop systems; neither directly measures the year-five operator-required share. The estimate treats physical slaughter, custody, sanitation, and accountable compliance as non-software service requirements and may overstate operator need if fully autonomous cells mature rapidly.
o.rationale: The service basket requires receiving live animals, humane slaughter, contamination control, carcass processing, cold-chain custody, packaging, labeling, and accountable regulatory compliance. Software can automate records and decisions and robotics can assist cuts, but demand is unlikely to become self-service or software-only within five years.
o.change_evidence: Regulatory acceptance records and production audits of highly automated slaughter lines showing which tasks, inspection interactions, corrective actions, and customer handoffs can operate without an accountable local processor.
caveats: Operator-required does not mean every current labor task persists.
State and federal inspection regimes differ, and custom-exempt product has restricted end use.

narrative.executive_view: The coherent opportunity is not commodity meatpacking broadly but small and midsize custom or toll processors that repeatedly serve livestock owners and food brands. Their physical, regulated workflow is durable, while practical AI value is concentrated in documentation, scheduling, sensing, quality alerts, and selective robotic assistance rather than wholesale labor replacement.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can reduce manual logs, monitor temperature and equipment, flag compliance deviations, optimize appointments and cut sheets, and assist vision-guided cutting. Variable carcasses, food-safety precision, humane-handling duties, washdown environments, and limited small-plant capital slow physical automation.
narrative.value_capture: Fee menus typically separate slaughter from per-pound processing and add charges for packaging or value-added work. That structure permits initial retention, but transparent local price lists, farmer bargaining, renewal repricing, and replicable software will share benefits with customers over time.
narrative.firm_availability: Only the custom and toll portion of LMM 311611 fits the recurring outsourced-service lens. Transfer supply is uncertain because aging ownership can create sales, but environmental, labor, inspection, facility, and livestock-supply risks can turn intended succession into closure or an asset transaction rather than a transferable going concern.
narrative.demand_durability: USDA projections support broadly stable-to-growing beef and pork consumption and pork exports. Physical slaughter and custody remain necessary, though cattle cycles, poultry substitution, integrated-plant share, and local livestock availability can make individual facility volumes volatile.
narrative.principal_driver: Durable regulated physical throughput paired with still-manual documentation, monitoring, scheduling, and selective production workflows.
narrative.principal_weakness: The eligible outsourced-service subset is not measured nationally and may be much smaller than the full LMM firm population.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are the national prevalence of fee-for-service revenue inside 311611, plant-level automation economics, and qualifying transfer rates. Food-safety incidents, humane-handling failures, wastewater constraints, labor scarcity, livestock shortages, and customer concentration can erase operational gains.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 311600
sources.supports: Broader-industry employment mix: 542,990 total jobs; food-processing workers 39.85%; cutters/trimmers 18.64%; slaughterers and meat packers 12.20%.

sources.title: Collaborative Cutting: Agile Robot Learners for Multipurpose Meat Industry Automation
sources.supports: USDA project states robotic uptake in U.S. meat and hoofstock facilities is limited because systems are expensive, single-purpose, and infrastructure-intensive, and describes human-in-the-loop robotic cutting.

sources.title: Edge Computing Driven Automation for Small and Medium Poultry and Meat Processors
sources.supports: USDA project describes manual logbook and clipboard data collection, physical equipment and temperature monitoring, and low-cost edge automation needs at small and midsize processors.

sources.title: Frequently Asked Questions About Processing and Marketing Your Own Meats
sources.supports: North Carolina agriculture guidance describes custom and inspected processing, repeat farm-processor relationships, labeling obligations, and the common flat slaughter fee plus per-pound processing charge.

sources.title: U.S. Pork Exports Projected to Surpass Chicken in the Next Decade
sources.supports: USDA projects pork exports rising from 6.95 billion pounds in 2024 to 9.34 billion in 2033 and 2033 domestic per-capita beef and pork consumption above 2024.

sources.title: Concentration in U.S. Meatpacking Industry and How It Affects Competition and Cattle Prices
sources.supports: USDA documents four-firm shares of 85% for steer/heifer purchases and 67% for hog purchases, scale economies, changing capacity, new entry, and price-spread behavior.

sources.title: Federal Meat Inspection Act
sources.supports: Statutory framework requires ante-mortem and post-mortem examination, humane slaughter, sanitary inspection, labeling, and other controls for meat establishments.
